content:
# Genesis Chapter 44 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 26r–26v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_044_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_044_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 44–45 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0048 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0049

---

*The Cup Hidden in Benjamin's Sack — Joseph's Command (vv. 1–2)*

**44:1** And he commanded [וַיְצַו] him who was over his house [אֶת-אֲשֶׁר עַל-בֵּיתוֹ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Fill [מַלֵּא] the men's sacks [אֶת-אַמְתְּחֹת הָאֲנָשִׁים] with food [אֹכֶל] as much as they are able to carry [כַּאֲשֶׁר יוּכְלוּן שְׂאֵת] — and place [וְשִׂים] each man's silver [כֶּסֶף-אִישׁ] in the mouth of his sack [בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ].

**44:2** And my goblet [וְאֶת-גְּבִיעִי] — the silver goblet [גְּבִיעַ הַכֶּסֶף] — place [תָּשִׂים] in the mouth of the sack of the youngest [בְּפִי אַמְתַּחַת הַקָּטֹן] and his grain silver [וְאֵת כֶּסֶף שִׁבְרוֹ] — and he did [וַיַּעַשׂ] according to the word of Joseph [כִּדְבַר יוֹסֵף] that he spoke [אֲשֶׁר דִּבֵּר].

---

*The Brothers Depart — The Steward Pursues Them (vv. 3–6)*

**44:3** The morning was light [הַבֹּקֶר אוֹר] and the men were sent away [וְהָאֲנָשִׁים שֻׁלְּחוּ] — they and their donkeys [הֵמָּה וַחֲמֹרֵיהֶם].

**44:4** They had gone out from the city [הֵם יָצְאוּ אֶת-הָעִיר] — they had not gone far [לֹא הִרְחִיקוּ] — and Joseph said [וְיוֹסֵף אָמַר] to him who was over his house [אֶל-אֲשֶׁר עַל-בֵּיתוֹ]: Arise [קוּם] — pursue the men [רְדֹף אַחֲרֵי הָאֲנָשִׁים] — and when you reach them [וְהִשַּׂגְתָּם] — say to them [וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם]: Why have you repaid evil for good [לָמָּה שִׁלַּמְתֶּם רָעָה תַּחַת טוֹבָה]?

**44:5** Is not this [הֲלוֹא זֶה] that which my lord drinks from [אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁתֶּה אֲדֹנִי בוֹ] — and he indeed practices divination with it [וְהוּא נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ בּוֹ] — you have done evil in what you have done [הֲרֵעֹתֶם אֲשֶׁר עֲשִׂיתֶם]?

**44:6** And he reached them [וַיַּשִּׂגֵם] and spoke to them [וַיְדַבֵּר אֲלֵהֶם] these words [אֶת-הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה].

---

*The Brothers' Protest — The Search — The Cup Found (vv. 7–12)*

**44:7** And they said to him [וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו]: Why does my lord speak [לָמָּה יְדַבֵּר אֲדֹנִי] these words [כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] — far be it from your servants [חָלִילָה לַעֲבָדֶיךָ] to do [מֵעֲשׂוֹת] such a thing [כַּדָּבָר הַזֶּה].

**44:8** Behold [הֵן] — the silver that we found [כֶּסֶף אֲשֶׁר מָצָאנוּ] in the mouth of our sacks [בְּפִי אַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ] — we brought back to you [הֱשִׁיבֹנוּ אֵלֶיךָ] from the land of Canaan [מֵאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — and how should we steal [וְאֵיךְ נִגְנֹב] from the house of your lord [מִבֵּית אֲדֹנֶיךָ] silver or gold [כֶּסֶף אוֹ זָהָב]?

**44:9** Whichever one of your servants [אֲשֶׁר יִמָּצֵא אִתּוֹ מֵעֲבָדֶיךָ] it is found with — he shall die [וָמֵת] — and also we [וְגַם-אֲנַחְנוּ] will become my lord's slaves [נִהְיֶה לַאדֹנִי לַעֲבָדִים].

**44:10** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Now also [גַּם-עַתָּה] so shall it be according to your words [כְדִבְרֵיכֶם כֵּן-הוּא] — the one with whom it is found [אֲשֶׁר יִמָּצֵא אִתּוֹ] — he shall become my slave [הוּא יִהְיֶה-לִּי עָבֶד] — and you [וְאַתֶּם] shall be blameless [תִּהְיוּ נְקִיִּם].

**44:11** And they hurried [וַיְמַהֲרוּ] and each man lowered [וַיּוֹרִדוּ אִישׁ] his sack to the ground [אֶת-אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ אָרְצָה] and each man opened [וַיִּפְתַּח אִישׁ] his sack [אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ].

**44:12** And he searched [וַיְחַפֵּשׂ] — with the eldest he began [בַּגָּדוֹל הֵחֵל] and with the youngest he finished [וּבַקָּטֹן כִּלָּה] — and the goblet was found [וַיִּמָּצֵא הַגָּבִיעַ] in Benjamin's sack [בְּאַמְתַּחַת בִּנְיָמִן].

---

*The Brothers Return to Joseph — Judah and Brothers Before Joseph (vv. 13–17)*

**44:13** And they tore their garments [וַיִּקְרְעוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם] and each man loaded [וַיַּעֲמֹס אִישׁ] upon his donkey [עַל-חֲמֹרוֹ] and they returned [וַיָּשֻׁבוּ] to the city [הָעִירָה].

**44:14** And Judah came [וַיָּבֹא יְהוּדָה] and his brothers [וְאֶחָיו] to Joseph's house [בֵּיתָה יוֹסֵף] — and he was still there [וְהוּא עוֹדֶנּוּ שָׁם] — and they fell before him [וַיִּפְּלוּ לְפָנָיו] to the ground [אָרְצָה].

**44:15** And Joseph said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם יוֹסֵף]: What is this deed [מָה-הַמַּעֲשֶׂה הַזֶּה] that you have done [אֲשֶׁר עֲשִׂיתֶם] — did you not know [הֲלוֹא יְדַעְתֶּם] that a man like me [כִּי-נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ] indeed practices divination [אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר כָּמֹנִי]?

**44:16** And Judah said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה]: What shall we say to my lord [מַה-נֹּאמַר לַאדֹנִי] — what shall we speak [מַה-נְּדַבֵּר] — and how shall we justify ourselves [וּמַה-נִּצְטַדָּק] — God has found out [הָאֱלֹהִים מָצָא] the iniquity of your servants [אֶת-עֲוֹן עֲבָדֶיךָ] — behold [הִנֶּנּוּ] — we are my lord's slaves [עֲבָדִים לַאדֹנִי] — both we [גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ] and he in whose hand the goblet was found [גַּם אֲשֶׁר-נִמְצָא הַגָּבִיעַ בְּיָדוֹ].

**44:17** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Far be it from me [חָלִילָה לִּי] to do this [מֵעֲשׂוֹת זֹאת] — the man in whose hand the goblet was found [הָאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר נִמְצָא הַגָּבִיעַ בְּיָדוֹ] — he shall be my slave [הוּא יִהְיֶה-לִּי עָבֶד] — and you [וְאַתֶּם] — go up in peace [עֲלוּ לְשָׁלוֹם] to your father [אֶל-אֲבִיכֶם].

---

*Judah's Great Speech — First Movement: Recounting What Was Said (vv. 18–23)*

**44:18** Then Judah drew near to him [וַיִּגַּשׁ אֵלָיו יְהוּדָה] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Please [בִּי] my lord [אֲדֹנִי] — let your servant speak [יְדַבֶּר-נָא עַבְדְּךָ] a word [דָבָר] in my lord's ears [בְּאָזְנֵי אֲדֹנִי] — and let not your anger burn [וְאַל-יִחַר אַפְּךָ] against your servant [בְּעַבְדֶּךָ] — for you are like Pharaoh [כִּי כָמוֹךָ כְּפַרְעֹה].

**44:19** My lord asked [אֲדֹנִי שָׁאַל] his servants [אֶת-עֲבָדָיו] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Is there to you a father [הֲיֵשׁ-לָכֶם אָב] or a brother [אוֹ-אָח]?

**44:20** And we said to my lord [וַנֹּאמֶר אֶל-אֲדֹנִי]: There is to us [יֶשׁ-לָנוּ] an aged father [אָב זָקֵן] and a child of his old age — the young one [וְיֶלֶד זְקֻנִים קָטָן] — and his brother is dead [וְאָחִיו מֵת] — and he alone remains [וַיִּוָּתֵר הוּא לְבַדּוֹ] of his mother [לְאִמּוֹ] — and his father loves him [וְאָבִיו אֲהֵבוֹ].

**44:21** And you said to your servants [וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל-עֲבָדֶיךָ]: Bring him down to me [הוֹרִידֻהוּ אֵלַי] — and I will set my eye upon him [וְאָשִׂימָה עֵינִי עָלָיו].

**44:22** And we said to my lord [וַנֹּאמֶר אֶל-אֲדֹנִי]: The youth is not able [לֹא-יוּכַל הַנַּעַר] to leave his father [לַעֲזֹב אֶת-אָבִיו] — for he would leave his father [כִּי-עָזַב אֶת-אָבִיו] and he would die [וָמֵת].

**44:23** And you said to your servants [וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל-עֲבָדֶיךָ]: If your youngest brother [אִם-לֹא יֵרֵד אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן] does not come down with you [אִתְּכֶם] — you shall not see my face again [לֹא תֹסִפוּן לִרְאוֹת פָּנָי].

---

*Judah's Great Speech — Second Movement: What Happened When We Returned (vv. 24–29)*

**44:24** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when we went up [כִּי עָלִינוּ] to your servant my father [אֶל-עַבְדְּךָ אָבִי] — and we told him [וַנַּגֶּד-לוֹ] the words of my lord [אֵת דִּבְרֵי אֲדֹנִי].

**44:25** And our father said [וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִינוּ]: Return [שֻׁבוּ] — buy for us a little food [שִׁבְרוּ-לָנוּ מְעַט-אֹכֶל].

**44:26** And we said [וַנֹּאמֶר]: We are not able [לֹא נוּכַל] to go down [לָרֶדֶת] — if our youngest brother [אִם-יֶשׁ אָחִינוּ הַקָּטֹן] is with us [אִתָּנוּ] we will go down [וְיָרַדְנוּ] — for we are not able [כִּי-לֹא נוּכַל] to see the man's face [לִרְאוֹת פְּנֵי הָאִישׁ] unless our youngest brother [וְאָחִינוּ הַקָּטֹן] is with us [אֵינֶנּוּ אִתָּנוּ].

**44:27** And your servant my father said to us [וַיֹּאמֶר עַבְדְּךָ אָבִי אֵלֵינוּ]: You know [אַתֶּם יְדַעְתֶּם] that my wife bore me [כִּי שְׁנַיִם יָלְדָה-לִּי אִשְׁתִּי] two [שְׁנַיִם].

**44:28** And the one went out from me [וַיֵּצֵא הָאֶחָד מֵאִתִּי] and I said [וָאֹמַר]: Surely he has been torn to pieces [אַךְ טָרֹף טֹרָף] — and I have not seen him since [וְלֹא רְאִיתִיו עַד-הֵנָּה].

**44:29** And if you take this one too [וּלְקַחְתֶּם גַּם-אֶת-זֶה] from my face [מֵעִם פָּנַי] and harm befalls him [וְקָרָהוּ אָסוֹן] — you will bring down my gray hair [וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם אֶת-שֵׂיבָתִי] in evil [בְּרָעָה] to Sheol [שְׁאֹלָה].

---

*Judah's Great Speech — Third Movement: The Surety Pledge — The Substitution Offer (vv. 30–34)*

**44:30** And now [וְעַתָּה] — when I come to your servant my father [כְּבֹאִי אֶל-עַבְדְּךָ אָבִי] and the youth is not with us [וְהַנַּעַר אֵינֶנּוּ אִתָּנוּ] — and his soul is bound up [וְנַפְשׁוֹ קְשׁוּרָה] with his soul [בְנַפְשׁוֹ] —

**44:31** it shall come to pass [וְהָיָה] when he sees [כִּרְאוֹתוֹ] that the youth is not there [כִּי-אֵין הַנַּעַר] — he will die [וָמֵת] — and your servants will bring down [וְהוֹרִידוּ עֲבָדֶיךָ] the gray hair of your servant our father [אֶת-שֵׂיבַת עַבְדְּךָ אָבִינוּ] in grief [בְּיָגוֹן] to Sheol [שְׁאֹלָה].

**44:32** For your servant [כִּי עַבְדְּךָ] took surety for the youth [עָרַב אֶת-הַנַּעַר] from my father [מֵעִם אָבִי] saying [לֵאמֹר]: If I do not bring him back to you [אִם-לֹא אֲבִיאֶנּוּ אֵלֶיךָ] — I will have sinned against my father [וְחָטָאתִי לְאָבִי] all the days [כָּל-הַיָּמִים].

**44:33** And now [וְעַתָּה] — let your servant remain [יֵשֶׁב-נָא עַבְדְּךָ] instead of the youth [תַּחַת הַנַּעַר] as a slave to my lord [עֶבֶד לַאדֹנִי] — and the youth [וְהַנַּעַר] — let him go up [יַעַל] with his brothers [עִם-אֶחָיו].

**44:34** For how shall I go up [כִּי-אֵיךְ אֶעֱלֶה] to my father [אֶל-אָבִי] and the youth is not with me [וְהַנַּעַר אֵינֶנּוּ אִתִּי] — lest I see [פֶּן אֶרְאֶה] the evil [בָּרָע] that will find my father [אֲשֶׁר יִמְצָא אֶת-אָבִי]?

---

## Philological Notes

### 44:2 — גְּבִיעַ הַכֶּסֶף (gevi'a ha-kesef)
"The silver goblet." The word גְּבִיעַ (gevi'a) appears elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible for the cups of Joseph's golden goblet-work in the tabernacle lampstand (Exod 25:31-36, the almond-blossom shaped cups of the menorah) and as a drinking vessel in Jer 35:5. It is a vessel of quality — not an ordinary clay cup but a formed, valuable drinking bowl, presumably of fine metalwork. The article (הַגָּבִיעַ, "the goblet") throughout the chapter treats it as a specific, known, unique object — Joseph's personal cup, immediately identifiable. The silver (כֶּסֶף) specifies the material; silver drinking vessels of exactly this type have been found in Egyptian archaeological deposits from the New Kingdom period. The cup has two functions in the narrative: (1) it is Joseph's personal drinking vessel, and therefore an object of intimate use and lordly honor, making its alleged theft a grave insult; (2) it is his divination instrument, which elevates the charge from theft to sacrilege.

### 44:5 — נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ (nachesh yenachesh)
"He indeed practices divination." This is the infinitive absolute construction par excellence — the same grammatical form used throughout Genesis for solemn emphasis (הָעֵד הֵעִד, "he solemnly warned," 43:3; טָרֹף טֹרָף, "he has been torn to pieces," 37:33). The root נָחַשׁ (nachash) means "to practice divination, to observe omens, to read signs." It appears in the same form in the lips of the steward (v.5) and of Joseph himself (v.15, where the word order is inverted: כִּי-נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר כָּמֹנִי — "for surely a man like me practices divination"). The double construction functions as: (a) a grammatical emphatic — "he truly, genuinely, habitually divines"; (b) a characterization of Joseph as a practitioner of Egyptian omen-reading, aligning him with the elite scribal-priestly class. Whether Joseph actually used the cup for divination or whether this is a persona he maintains is left deliberately ambiguous by the narrator. The cup's function as a divination instrument gives the brothers' apparent theft an even darker coloring: they have not merely stolen property, they have stolen sacred ritual apparatus. LXX renders τοῦτό ἐστιν ἐν ᾧ πίνει ὁ κύριός μου, αὐτὸς δὲ οἰωνισμῷ οἰωνίζεται ἐν αὐτῷ — "this is that in which my lord drinks, and he practices augury in it" — using οἰωνισμός (augury from birds/omens), the standard Greek divination term.

### 44:13 — וַיִּקְרְעוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם (vayyiqre'u simlotam)
"They tore their garments." The verb קָרַע (qara', "to tear") with the object שִׂמְלָה (simlah, "outer garment, cloak") is the formal act of mourning and anguish in the Hebrew Bible. Jacob himself tore his garments when the bloodied coat of many colors was brought to him: וַיִּקְרַע יַעֲקֹב שִׂמְלֹתָיו (37:34) — the exact same verb and object in the singular. The narrator's echo is unmistakable: Jacob tore his garments in grief over the apparent loss of Joseph; now Joseph's brothers tear their garments in grief over what appears to be the loss of Benjamin. The verb form here is Qal imperfect with waw-consecutive third masculine plural — "and they tore" — a single decisive action by all the brothers simultaneously, not one by one. The tearing is total: all eleven brothers, together, in the road outside the city. The moral symmetry is exquisite. The coat whose false-bloodying caused Jacob's tearing in 37:34 is now echoed in the brothers' tearing in 44:13 — and it will be resolved only when Jacob tears no more, in 45:27-28 when he learns his son is alive.

### 44:16 — הָאֱלֹהִים מָצָא אֶת-עֲוֹן עֲבָדֶיךָ (ha-Elohim matsa et-avon avadekha)
"God has found out the iniquity of your servants." This is the theological climax of Judah's first response — and one of the most theologically resonant lines in all of Genesis. Judah does not say "we are innocent" — he says the opposite: God has found us out. The word עָוֹן (avon) is not a light word — it means "guilt, iniquity, the moral weight of wrongdoing" — from a root meaning "to be bent, twisted." Judah is not confessing specifically to the theft of the cup (which he knows they did not do). He is confessing to something deeper: the unspecified guilt that has been riding on the brothers since the beginning of the Egypt journeys. The reader knows what Judah means, and perhaps Judah himself knows — this is the guilt of what was done to Joseph, now surfacing through an inexplicable series of events that feel like providential exposure. The God-language here is significant: Judah does not say "you found us out" — he says God found us out. He is attributing Joseph's seemingly magical discovery of the cup to divine action, not to any natural ability of the Egyptian lord. The phrase echoes Num 32:23 — "be sure your sin will find you out" — where the same root מָצָא (matsa) is used. LXX renders ὁ θεὸς εὗρεν τὴν ἀδικίαν τῶν παίδων σου — "God found the injustice of your servants" — using εὑρίσκω (to find, to discover) and ἀδικία (injustice), preserving the theological attribution perfectly.

### 44:20 — וְיֶלֶד זְקֻנִים קָטָן (veleled zekunim qatan)
"A child of old age — the young one." The phrase יֶלֶד זְקֻנִים (yeled zekunim) is the same phrase used of Joseph himself in 37:3: בֶּן-זְקֻנִים הוּא לוֹ (ben-zekunim hu lo, "a son of old age to him"). Judah, speaking before Joseph without knowing it, describes Benjamin using the exact same language that the narrative used to describe Joseph — "a child of old age." The verbal echo is pointed: the man before whom Judah stands, pleading for the child-of-old-age, is himself the first child-of-old-age, the one Judah once proposed selling. The irony accumulates layer upon layer. Benjamin is described as קָטָן (qatan, "young, small, the youngest") — a word that echoes throughout these chapters wherever the brothers speak of him to Joseph (43:29, 44:2, 44:12, etc.). Benjamin's vulnerability is inscribed in his epithet.

### 44:22 — כִּי-עָזַב אֶת-אָבִיו וָמֵת (ki-azav et-aviv vamet)
"For if he leaves his father, he would die." This clause is ambiguous in a way that cuts in multiple directions. The implied subject of "and he would die" (וָמֵת) is either the father or the son — and both readings are devastatingly true. If the father is the implied subject: Jacob would die if Benjamin left him (see 42:38, 44:29, 44:31 — Jacob's death is the consistent threat associated with Benjamin leaving). If the son is the subject: Benjamin would die away from his father's protection (an implicit claim about the dangerous world outside Canaan). Most commentators take the subject as the father (Jacob); but the ambiguity is deliberate. The grammar is a third masculine singular: "and he would die" — which grammatically most naturally refers back to the father (אָב) mentioned in the preceding clause. The statement is Judah's indirect way of saying: our father's life and our brother's life are intertwined to the point of identity.

### 44:27-28 — שְׁנַיִם יָלְדָה-לִּי אִשְׁתִּי... אַךְ טָרֹף טֹרָף (shnayim yaldah-li ishti... akh tarof toraf)
"My wife bore me two... Surely he has been torn to pieces." Judah quotes Jacob's words to Joseph — again without knowing it. Jacob's statement that his wife bore him two (not twelve) makes a stunning implicit hierarchy: of all twelve sons, only the two sons of Rachel count as "his wife's sons" in the deepest emotional sense. This is not an objective genealogical statement but a statement of grief and love — the two sons who filled his life uniquely. The phrase אַךְ טָרֹף טֹרָף (akh tarof toraf) — "surely he has been torn to pieces" — is the infinitive absolute of טָרַף (taraf, "to tear, to be torn by a wild animal"), the same form used by the brothers themselves when they showed Jacob the coat: הַכֶּר-נָא... טָרֹף טֹרַף יוֹסֵף (37:33, "a wild animal has indeed torn Joseph"). Jacob's grief-language, forged by the brothers' own deception, is now quoted back by Judah to the very man whose death it described. Joseph is listening to his own epitaph quoted by the man who arranged his "death."

### 44:30-31 — וְנַפְשׁוֹ קְשׁוּרָה בְנַפְשׁוֹ (venahsho qeshurah benahsho)
"And his soul is bound up with his soul." This is one of the most poignant phrases in the entire Joseph narrative. The verb קָשַׁר (qashar, "to bind, to tie") in the passive participle describes the bond between Jacob's soul (נֶפֶשׁ, nephesh) and Benjamin's soul as something already accomplished — an existing, permanent tie. The word נֶפֶשׁ (nephesh) in classical Hebrew means the whole person, the self, the life, the animating breath — not merely the soul in a Platonic sense but the entire vital existence of a human being. Judah says: the old man's existence is tied to the boy's existence. They are not two independent lives — they are one life distributed between two bodies. The force of this for the scene: Judah is not merely saying his father will be sad. He is saying his father will cease to exist. And the word וָמֵת (vamet, "and he will die") in verse 31 confirms it — if the boy is not there, the father dies. Joseph is hearing Judah describe the structure of his own father's love — a love that held on to Benjamin precisely because Joseph was gone.

### 44:32 — כִּי עַבְדְּךָ עָרַב אֶת-הַנַּעַר (ki avdekha arav et-hana'ar)
"For your servant took surety for the youth." The verb עָרַב (arav, "to pledge oneself as surety") is the same verb Judah used in 43:9 when he made his pledge to Jacob: אָנֹכִי אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ ("I myself will be surety for him"). There Judah made the pledge to his father; here he invokes that pledge before Joseph as the legal ground for his substitution offer. The structure is: pledge made (43:9) → pledge recalled (44:32) → pledge fulfilled (44:33). Judah's offer of substitution is not a spontaneous act of nobility — it is the logical, even obligatory completion of a formal surety commitment. He cannot return to his father without Benjamin; if he cannot return with Benjamin, he must return as something else — namely, as Benjamin's replacement in servitude. The juridical precision of Judah's speech is part of its rhetorical power: this is not emotion only, but law, and the law demands what the emotion desires.

### 44:33 — יֵשֶׁב-נָא עַבְדְּךָ תַּחַת הַנַּעַר עֶבֶד לַאדֹנִי (yeshev-na avdekha tachat hana'ar eved la'adoni)
"Let your servant remain instead of the youth as a slave to my lord." This is the moral apex of the Joseph narrative and the theological center of the entire Judah arc. The particle נָא (na') — "please, I beg you, I pray" — turns the indicative into a petition of utmost urgency. The verb יָשַׁב (yashav, "to remain, to dwell, to stay") in this context means "to stay behind, to be detained" — Judah is asking to be left as a slave in Benjamin's place. The preposition תַּחַת (tachat, "instead of, beneath, in place of") is the word used throughout the Hebrew Bible for substitution — both commercial (paying one thing in place of another) and sacrificial (one life offered in place of another). Here it is a fully deliberate self-substitution: Judah, the son of Leah, offers himself as permanent slave in place of Benjamin, the son of Rachel. The man who in 37:26 asked "what profit is it if we kill our brother?" and proposed selling him as a slave has become the man who offers himself as a slave to protect his brother. The transformation is complete. The same word עֶבֶד (eved, "slave") that Joseph became, Judah now offers himself to become. LXX renders μενέτω ὁ παῖς σου ἀντὶ τοῦ παιδίου οἰκέτης τοῦ κυρίου — "let your servant remain instead of the child as a household slave to the lord" — using οἰκέτης (household slave) for the more specific domestic servitude, and ἀντί (instead of) for תַּחַת.

### 44:34 — פֶּן אֶרְאֶה בָּרָע אֲשֶׁר יִמְצָא אֶת-אָבִי (pen er'eh bara' asher yimtsa et-avi)
"Lest I see the evil that will find my father." The final word of Judah's speech is one of the most devastating lines in Genesis. The word רָע (ra') — "evil, harm, disaster" — is the same word used when the brothers returned with the bloodied coat and Jacob said "Joseph is certainly torn to pieces" and then "he refused to be comforted... and he mourned for his son many days" (37:34-35). The verb מָצָא (matsa, "to find, to meet, to come upon") with רָע as subject is the idiom for "evil befall him" — but here it is rendered almost personified: the evil that will find my father, as if disaster is a hunter and Jacob is its prey. The verb אֶרְאֶה (er'eh, "I will see") in the first person carries the full weight: Judah cannot bear to watch. He cannot bear to be present when the man who has already lost one son discovers he has lost another. The speech ends not with heroic self-declaration but with a confession of what Judah cannot endure. It is this — more than any legal argument, more than any pledge — that breaks Joseph.

---

## LXX Comparison Table (Vaticanus, pp. 44–45)

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus Greek) | Significant Divergence |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 44:2 | גְּבִיעַ הַכֶּסֶף — "the silver goblet" | τὸ κόνδυ τὸ ἀργυροῦν — "the silver cup/vessel" | LXX uses κόνδυ, a loanword from Persian/Egyptian for a drinking vessel; MT's גְּבִיעַ is a native Hebrew term for a quality cup |
| 44:4 | לָמָּה שִׁלַּמְתֶּם רָעָה תַּחַת טוֹבָה — "why have you repaid evil for good?" | ἵνα τί ἀνταπεδώκατε πονηρὰ ἀντὶ καλῶν — "why did you repay evil things instead of good things?" | LXX uses ἀνταποδίδωμι (to repay in return), capturing the reciprocal quality of the verb שִׁלַּם; near-exact equivalence |
| 44:5 | נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ בּוֹ — "he indeed practices divination with it" | αὐτὸς δὲ οἰωνισμῷ οἰωνίζεται ἐν αὐτῷ — "and he practices augury with it" | LXX uses οἰωνισμός (augury from bird-omens), the closest Greek category; does not fully preserve the infinitive absolute emphasis; MT's double construction (נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ) emphasizes habitual/certain practice |
| 44:9 | וָמֵת — "he shall die" | ἀποθανέτω — "let him die" | MT is a simple perfect/waw-consecutive (declarative: "he shall die"); LXX uses imperative (ἀποθανέτω, "let him die") — stronger, more self-condemnatory tone in LXX |
| 44:13 | וַיִּקְרְעוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם — "they tore their garments" | διέρρηξαν τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτῶν — "they tore their garments" | LXX uses διαρρήγνυμι ("to tear apart thoroughly"), a strong compound verb; exact semantic equivalence; the intertextual echo to 37:34 (Jacob's tearing) is present in both MT and LXX |
| 44:16 | הָאֱלֹהִים מָצָא אֶת-עֲוֹן עֲבָדֶיךָ — "God has found out the iniquity of your servants" | ὁ θεὸς εὗρεν τὴν ἀδικίαν τῶν παίδων σου — "God found the injustice of your servants" | LXX uses εὑρίσκω (to find/discover) and ἀδικία (injustice/wrongdoing); MT's עָוֹן (iniquity/guilt) has stronger moral/theological weight than ἀδικία; otherwise exact |
| 44:20 | וְיֶלֶד זְקֻנִים קָטָן — "a child of old age, the young one" | παιδίον γήρως νεώτερον — "a child of old age, the younger one" | LXX renders with exact equivalence; the echo of Joseph's own description (בֶּן-זְקֻנִים, 37:3) is present in both texts |
| 44:28 | אַךְ טָרֹף טֹרָף — "surely he has been torn to pieces" | Θηριόβρωτος γέγονεν — "he has become devoured by a wild beast" | LXX abandons the infinitive absolute construction and uses a compound adjective (θηριόβρωτος, "wild-beast-eaten"); loses the emphatic doubling of MT but gains a vivid compound term |
| 44:30 | וְנַפְשׁוֹ קְשׁוּרָה בְנַפְשׁוֹ — "and his soul is bound up with his soul" | τῆς δὲ ψυχῆς αὐτοῦ ἠρτημένης ἐκ τῆς τούτου ψυχῆς — "and his soul hanging from this one's soul" | LXX uses ἀρτάω ("to hang, to be suspended from") — a slightly different metaphor from MT's קָשַׁר ("to bind, tie"); LXX "hanging" implies dependence/suspension; MT "bound" implies interlocking unity |
| 44:31 | שֵׂיבַת עַבְדְּךָ אָבִינוּ בְּיָגוֹן שְׁאֹלָה — "the gray hair of your servant our father in grief to Sheol" | τὴν γῆρας τοῦ παιδός σου πατρὸς ἡμῶν μετ᾽ ὀδύνης εἰς ᾅδου — "the old age of your servant our father with pain into Hades" | LXX renders שְׁאֹל (Sheol) as ᾅδης (Hades), its standard equivalent; MT's שֵׂיבָה (gray hair) is the synecdoche for old age; LXX uses γῆρας (old age) directly — less vivid but clearer |
| 44:33 | יֵשֶׁב-נָא עַבְדְּךָ תַּחַת הַנַּעַר עֶבֶד לַאדֹנִי — "let your servant remain instead of the youth as a slave to my lord" | μενέτω ὁ παῖς σου ἀντὶ τοῦ παιδίου οἰκέτης τοῦ κυρίου μου — "let your servant remain instead of the child as a household slave to my lord" | LXX uses οἰκέτης (household slave, domestic servant) for MT's simple עֶבֶד (slave); the particle נָא ("please/I pray") is not separately rendered in LXX; ἀντί captures תַּחַת ("instead of") precisely |
| 44:34 | פֶּן אֶרְאֶה בָּרָע אֲשֶׁר יִמְצָא אֶת-אָבִי — "lest I see the evil that will find my father" | ἵνα μὴ ἴδω τὰ κακὰ τὰ εὑρήσοντά τὸν πατέρα μου — "lest I see the evils that will find my father" | LXX renders the singular רָע as the plural τὰ κακά ("the evils"), perhaps to capture the manifold nature of the disaster; otherwise exact equivalence |

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 44 is the chapter of the final test — the net that Joseph lays for his brothers, and the speech in which Judah walks into the net and tears it open from the inside with the force of his own moral transformation.

**The architecture of the chapter:** The chapter is built in two unequal halves, with the break at verse 17. The first half (vv.1-17) is the mechanism of the test: the concealed cup, the pursuit, the accusation, the search, the finding, the return, and Joseph's offer of conditional release. The second half (vv.18-34) is Judah's great speech — a single extended oration that occupies 17 of the chapter's 34 verses, making it the longest uninterrupted speech in Genesis. The structural imbalance is itself expressive: the elaborate mechanical trap of the first half is overwhelmed by the human testimony of the second. Joseph's test is answered not with logic but with love, not with legal argument but with moral exposure.

**The cup as theological instrument:** The goblet (גְּבִיעַ) is doubly significant. As Joseph's personal drinking vessel, it represents his intimate self — something taken from the inside of his household. As a divination instrument, it represents divine authority — the ability to see hidden things. The irony of the test is that the cup placed in Benjamin's sack is ostensibly a sacred divination tool, and through the "divination" of that cup Joseph will indeed see something hidden: whether his brothers have changed. The cup does not divine by magic — it divines by revealing what is in human hearts when confronted with extremity.

**The brothers' self-condemnation in verse 9:** When the brothers declare "whichever one of your servants it is found with — he shall die — and we will be your slaves," they unknowingly offer a judgment heavier than Joseph imposes. The self-condemnation echoes Rachel's fatal oath in 31:32 ("Whoever you find your gods with shall not live") — another unconscious self-curse that the narrator records without comment. Joseph's counter-offer (v.10: only the one found with it shall be my slave; you shall be blameless) is actually more merciful than the brothers' own proposal.

**Judah's speech as five movements:** Judah's speech in vv.18-34 can be analyzed in five rhetorical movements:
1. *The appeal for a hearing* (v.18): "Please, my lord — let your servant speak a word in my lord's ears — for you are like Pharaoh." The comparison to Pharaoh establishes Joseph's supreme authority — Judah appeals to that authority rather than challenging it.
2. *The narrative of what was asked* (vv.19-23): Judah recounts the entire history of Joseph's questioning — the inquiry about father and brothers, the revelation of Benjamin's existence, the demand that Benjamin come, the warning about seeing Joseph's face. By retelling history from his perspective, Judah forces Joseph to hear his own actions narrated by someone who has no idea of the full story.
3. *The narrative of what happened when they returned* (vv.24-29): Judah recounts Jacob's reluctance, his own insistence, and above all Jacob's words — including the devastating quotation of "surely he has been torn to pieces" (v.28) and the forecast of Jacob's death if Benjamin does not return (v.29).
4. *The invocation of the surety pledge* (vv.30-32): Having established the human stakes, Judah invokes the formal legal ground of his offer — his personal surety pledge to his father. This is not sentiment; it is obligation.
5. *The self-substitution offer and its ground* (vv.33-34): The climax. Judah offers himself as slave instead of Benjamin — and the reason he gives is not "I love Benjamin" or even "I pledged myself." It is: "lest I see the evil that will find my father." The last word of his speech is not about Judah — it is about his father.

**The reversal of chapter 37:** The structural inversion of Genesis 44 and Genesis 37 is the chapter's deepest theological statement. In 37:26-27, Judah proposed selling Joseph as a slave and profiting from his absence: "What profit is it if we kill our brother... come, let us sell him." In 44:33, Judah offers himself as a slave and refuses to profit from Benjamin's absence. The same man, the same verb (עֶבֶד, slave), the same family dynamic (a favored younger brother, a grief-destroyed father) — and a completely inverted moral response. Between chapter 37 and chapter 44 stands chapter 38 — Judah's encounter with Tamar, in which he was exposed and declared "she is more righteous than I." That humiliation and moral confrontation planted the seed of the transformation that flowers in chapter 44's speech. Judah becomes righteous by first being shown his unrighteousness.

**The soul-bond (v.30) and its theological resonance:** Judah's phrase "his soul is bound up with his soul" (וְנַפְשׁוֹ קְשׁוּרָה בְנַפְשׁוֹ) is the most intimate statement in the chapter. The image of two souls tied together is not merely poetic hyperbole — it is a description of what love does to the self. Jacob's identity has been reorganized around Benjamin's survival after Joseph's loss. Judah communicates this to the very man whose loss created the reorganization. Joseph is hearing, from his brother's lips, what his own disappearance did to his father — and learning that what remained of his father's life has been poured into Benjamin. This is what breaks Joseph in the next chapter (45:1). Judah's speech is not primarily an argument. It is a disclosure. And what it discloses — the depth of his father's suffering, the reality of the family's grief, the completeness of his own moral conversion — is more than Joseph can withstand.

**"For you are like Pharaoh" (v.18):** Judah's opening comparison — "you are like Pharaoh" (כְּפַרְעֹה) — is diplomatically essential but narratively ironic. Judah is telling the truth in a way he does not know: this man before him is indeed like Pharaoh in that he holds absolute power over Egypt's grain and over the lives of those who come before him. But Joseph is also — as the reader knows — not like Pharaoh in the deepest sense: he is Judah's own brother, and the power he holds is a divine setup, not an Egyptian birthright. The comparison collapses in the next chapter (45:1-3) when Joseph sends the Egyptians out and says "I am Joseph." He is not like Pharaoh. He is their brother.
